---
type: primer
provenance: "synthesis"
---

# The Shape of Time {.primer .unnumbered}

*Silhouette: One question runs through every scale of a designed experience: how much of its shape does the author fix, and how much does the player find for themselves?*

<!-- Primer: orientation to the Structure & Pacing family. Light authorial / Mentor voice. -->

## The Terrain

This family is about the shape an experience takes over its whole length, and how that shape is felt minute to minute. The two are not the same thing. Structure is the shape. It says where the story begins, how its pieces are joined, where it stops. Pacing is the felt rhythm. It is how heavy a scene sits in the moment, how fast the last hour runs, whether attention is climbing or flagging by the end. A piece can have a sound shape and a dead rhythm, or a thrilling rhythm hung on a shape that collapses on reflection. The craft is holding both. [synthesis]

Underneath both sits a single question, and it returns at every scale. At the beat, the scene, the arc, the whole campaign, the designer keeps facing the same choice. How much of the shape do I fix in advance, and how much does the player get to find? Fix all of it and the player rides a track, comfortable but never free. Fix none of it and they are left in an open field with nothing to push against. The good answer is never one of the poles. It is a position taken on purpose, and that position can shift from one scale to the next. A single piece might run a tightly authored scene inside an otherwise wide-open campaign. It might hand the player the order of events while the events themselves stay fixed. [synthesis]

What makes this an engineering question rather than a matter of taste is cost. Every branch a designer grants has to be built, and the branches multiply. Three binary choices already demand eight paths to honour them fully; ten demand more than a thousand. Nobody writes a thousand endings well. Freedom is neither free nor infinite. It is a budget, spent where it shows and saved where it does not. The whole family, from the smallest fork in a passage up to the open-table campaign that holds a different group every week, is a set of techniques for buying the freedom that matters and declining to pay for the freedom that does not. [synthesis]

::: {.callout-tip}
### Mentor Wisdom
When a structure feels expensive, the instinct is to cut a branch. Sometimes that is right. More often the cheaper fix is to let the paths rejoin. A choice that diverges and then converges still felt real in the moment it was made, and the reader rarely audits whether the roads met again downstream. What they cannot forgive is a choice that never mattered at all. So spend your divergence on the moment of choosing and the consequence that follows it, then bring the paths quietly back together before the cost compounds. Converging is not cheating. Pretending a choice mattered when it did not is the only cheat the player actually catches. [synthesis]
:::

## Key Mechanisms

The family runs from the smallest rhythm up to the longest shape, so this is the order to read it in.

**The felt rhythm, beat by beat.** Below every branch is a heartbeat. A setback, then a breath in which the player decides what to do next, repeating for the length of the piece: that is [Scene & Sequel in Interactive Fiction](../articles/structure-pacing/scene-and-sequel-in-interactive-fiction.qmd), the micro-rhythm where most choices actually live. The unit those beats are built and cut in, along with the seams between them, is [Scene Structure & Transitions](../articles/structure-pacing/scene-structure-and-transitions.qmd). The engine that keeps a sequence of setbacks from reading as repetition, turning each failure into the reason for the next, is [Try-Fail Cycles & Escalation](../articles/structure-pacing/try-fail-cycles-and-escalation.qmd). And the art of spending a whole group's attention so the last hour is the best one is [Session Pacing & Energy Management](../articles/structure-pacing/session-pacing-and-energy-management.qmd).

**The shape at the scale of the whole.** To name what each story moment is for, the field borrows maps from screen and page: [Beat Taxonomies & Craft Models](../articles/structure-pacing/beat-taxonomies-and-craft-models.qmd) catalogues them and draws the line the linear models never had to, between a beat the structure needs everywhere and a beat a branch is free to vary. The last beat of all, where the story stops being a path and becomes a thing the player will retell, is [Endings & Structural Closure](../articles/structure-pacing/endings-and-structural-closure.qmd).

**Branching, where the cost question is sharpest.** The anchor of the family is [Branching Narrative Craft](../articles/structure-pacing/branching-narrative-craft.qmd): how to make a choice cost something without making the story cost everything. This is where the freedom budget is spent or wasted. Its companion is the shape-catalogue, [IF Graph Structures: A Taxonomy](../articles/structure-pacing/if-graph-structures-a-taxonomy.qmd), which reads a branching story's silhouette straight off its graph and sets each shape's gains against its costs. The arithmetic that tells you whether the shape you want is affordable, worked out before you build it, is [Scope, Scale & Branching Depth](../articles/structure-pacing/scope-scale-and-branching-depth.qmd). The same trade-off in plywood rather than prose, where a path not built still costs a real set, is [Escape Room Topologies](../articles/structure-pacing/escape-room-topologies.qmd), so a physical room favours shapes that let many hands work in parallel and then funnel back to one finish.

**Nonlinear and temporal forms.** Order itself is a structural variable. Telling a story out of sequence so the reader does the assembling, without losing them, is [Nonlinear Narrative Structures](../articles/structure-pacing/nonlinear-narrative-structures.qmd). Rewinding the world but not the person, so the only thing that moves forward is what the character knows, is [Time Loop Patterns](../articles/structure-pacing/time-loop-patterns.qmd). Running several stories apart long enough for the reader to feel them snap together is [Parallel Threads & Convergence](../articles/structure-pacing/parallel-threads-and-convergence.qmd). And cutting one arc into instalments that arrive on a calendar, each whole enough to satisfy and open enough to pull the reader back, is [Episodic & Serialised Structure](../articles/structure-pacing/episodic-and-serialised-structure.qmd).

**Campaign and live formats.** At the longest scale the shape has to survive months of real evenings. The choice of form, weighed against how much a referee must prepare and how much the players steer, is [Campaign Arc Shapes](../articles/structure-pacing/campaign-arc-shapes.qmd). The axis the author-fixes-versus-player-finds question is named on most directly, running from a track you ride to a world you push against, is [Sandbox, Situation & the Railroad Spectrum](../articles/structure-pacing/sandbox-situation-and-the-railroad-spectrum.qmd). When the map itself does the work of plot, a space you can walk in any direction, the form is [Hexcrawl & Pointcrawl](../articles/structure-pacing/hexcrawl-and-pointcrawl.qmd). And a persistent world that holds together even when a different group shows up every time, because the players rather than the calendar decide when to play, is [West Marches & the Open-Table Format](../articles/structure-pacing/west-marches-and-the-open-table-format.qmd).

## Related Exemplars

*Groundhog Day* (1993) is the time loop in its purest popular form, the world resetting while only Phil's knowledge carries forward. *Pulp Fiction* (1994) braids three stories that share a city and a grain until they fold into one shape. Dwight Swain's *Techniques of the Selling Writer* (1965) gave the scene-and-sequel rhythm the vocabulary the field still uses. And Ben Robbins's West Marches, run from 2001 to 2003 and written up in 2007, is the open-table campaign proven in practice: a persistent world that stayed coherent while the roster changed every session.
